teamObjective.explanation: Partner has only 2 cards and is near finishing, so the best opening lead is a low single that partner can likely beat or that forces opponents to spend a high card, transferring initiative toward the finishing partner.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner likely has one or two relatively strong cards since they are down to 2 cards; they probably want a cheap path to win a trick and lead out their last card.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents hold 3 cards each and have seen no cards played yet; they are likely to conserve high cards unless forced, so a low lead may be allowed to pass or be taken cheaply by a middling card.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the 3 of diamonds is the cheapest sacrifice; it maximizes the chance that partner can win the trick with a modest card while keeping my 4/5/6 for later tricks if I regain the lead.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: Leading the 6 expends my highest non-trump single and is still easily beaten by any 7, trump, or higher card; it weakens my hand without increasing the chance of partner winning.
The 5 is middling and could directly lose to partner's winning card or force partner to use a higher card than necessary; preserving it is better.
The 4 is only slightly higher than the 3 and still weak; playing the 3 instead preserves all stronger cards and is the most economical lead.

risks: Partner may be unable to beat whatever opponent plays and we lose the lead.
An opponent may play a trump or high card to seize initiative.
My remaining low cards may not win any future trick if I need to finish.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By keeping 4/5/6 and leading the weakest card, I limit information leakage and retain cards that can potentially beat similarly low future leads or be played in sequence.
